0: Comparison of greenhouse gas emissions from corn- and barley-based dairy production systems in Eastern Canada
Jessie Guyader Shannan Little Roland Kröbel Chaouki Benchaar Karen A. Beauchemin a,*
a Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada, Lethbridge Research and Development Centre, Lethbridge, Alberta T1J 4B1, Canada
b Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada, Sherbrooke Research and Development Centre, Sherbrooke, Québec J1M 0C8, Canada

A R T I C INFO|ABSTRACT Article history: Received 16 May 2016 Received in revised form 2 December 2016 Accepted 2 December 2016 Available online 22 December 2016 Keywords: Barley silage Corn silage Dairy production system Digestibility Greenhouse gas Life cycle analysis|"In Canada, corn silage is increasingly fed to lactating dairy cows at the expense of barley silage and other forages, as its high-energy content can improve animal performance. Moreover, corn silage is known to reduce methanogenesis in the rumen compared to barley silage. A life cycle analysis was conducted to compare whole farm total GHG emission and greenhouse gas (GHG) intensity (kilogram CO2-equivalent per kilogram of milk) of corn- (CS) and barley- (BS) based dairy production systems. For this purpose, a virtual farm representative of typical dairy production systems in Quebec was used to simulate the 6-year lifespan of a dairy cow, from calv- ing to culling. Diets fed to lactating cows consisted of 54.4% corn or barley silage, 5.5% grass hay and 40.1% con- centrate (dry matter basis). The impact of silage digestibility (measured as total digestible nutrient [TDN] content) on total GHG emissions of the dairy production system was also assessed. From prior experimental data, milk production was assumed to average 34.7 and 31.9 kg/day for lactating COWS fed corn and barley silages of medium TDN content respectively. Milk production was also assumed to be positively correlated with the TDN content of diets. To compensate for differences in milk production per cow, the number of COWS was adjusted to obtain similar total fat- and protein-corrected milk production between farms. Forage (silage and hay) and grain (barley or corn) were cultivated on-farm whereas all other feed ingredients were purchased. Greenhouse gas emissions were estimated with the Holos model using a ""cradle-to-farm gate"" approach. Methane (enteric fer- mentation and manure storage), CO2 (farm operations, production and transportation of purchased feed) and N20 (N degradation from crop residue, manure, N leaching and volatilization) emissions were taken into account. Enteric CH4 was predicted from animal energy requirements and diet composition. Percentage of energy intake lost as CH4 was assumed constant regardless of silage TDN content. When silages having medium TDN content were used, total GHG emission was reduced by 13% with CS compared to BS, despite the fact that the reduction of enteric CH4 emissions with corn silage was partially offset by increased CO2 emissions from the additional pur- chased feed protein sources (+9%). Within a forage type, increasing silage TDN content reduced GHG intensity. Finally, the GHG intensity of dairy production systems was lower with high digestible barley silage compared to low digestible corn silage showing the importance of producing forages with high digestibility that maximize milk production. 1: Ruminants are an important food source in human diets, supplying almost the totality of milk products and one third of meat consumed globally (Gerber et al., 2013b). Unlike monogastric animals, ruminants can make use of forages because of their unique digestive system, reduc- ing the competition for grain used in human nutrition and maintaining various ecosystem services associated with forage cropping (Guyader et

al., 2016). Corn and barley silages are the main annual forage sources in Canada. Because of a shorter growing season, barley silage is mostly pro- duced in Western Canada whereas corn silage is widely grown in East- ern Canada. With climatic warming and the development of short- season corn hybrids, corn silage cultivation areas are progressively in- creasing throughout the country (Addah et al., 2011). Corn silage is par- ticularly attractive to farmers as it improves performance of animals due to its higher total digestible nutrient (TDN) content in comparison to barley silage attributed to its higher starch content (20-40% of DM for corn silage, Bal et al., 1997; 10-30% of DM for barley silage, Hargreaves et al., 2009). Also, it is well documented that high-starch diets reduce methane (CH4) production from ruminants as shown by

In Canada, enteric CH4 production by ruminants accounts for 38% of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions within the agricultural sector (Environment Canada, 2012). Modification of forage source is among the best potential strategies to improve CH4 efficiency of ruminants, expressed as the ratio between CH4 and food production (Gerber et al., 2013a). Indeed, unlike some direct CH4-mitigating agents, modifica- tion of dietary composition offers the double advantage of reducing CH4 production while improving production efficiency. Moreover, on-farm adoption of alternative forages and forage harvesting management are relatively simple, albeit limited by local agronomic constraints, in con- trast to feeding CH4-suppressants that are experimental and not yet reg- istered for feeding commercially. However, modification of silage source requires a major shift in the cropping system of the farm.

Thus, prior to recommending corn rather than a barley feeding sys- tem as a CH4 mitigation strategy, it is essential to assess the net impact of corn-based dairy production systems on GHG intensity of milk pro- duction to ensure that corn silage does not reduce enteric CH4 emissions at the expense of other GHG sources (carbon dioxide [CO2] and nitrous oxide [N2O]). In addition, this study is the first to report the GHG inten- sity of a barley-based dairy production system using barley silage as the main forage source. Therefore, the objective of this life cycle assessment (LCA) was to compare the GHG intensity of milk production of a corn- based dairy production system (CS) with a barley-based system (BS). As nutrient content and yields of forages are subject to environmental conditions including geographical location and climate (Bal et al., 1997; Hargreaves et al., 2009), the effects of varying silage TDN content and biomass yield of these two forages on GHG intensity of dairy pro- duction systems was also explored.

5: Input data were based on the study of Benchaar et al. (2014) con- ducted in Quebec, thus the model dairy farms were located at Napierville in the Montérégie region, Quebec, Canada with a fine loam soil texture cultivated under reduced tillage practice. The farms were lo- cated in Ecodistrict 541 in the Mixed Wood Plains Ecozone. Precipitation and evapotranspiration during the growing season (May-October) av- eraged 559 and 529 mm, respectively (Marshall et al., 1999). The LCA followed the baseline scenario for the dairy production system in this province developed by Mc Geough et al. (2012).

1: The LCA started with the birth of 40-kg female calves that succes- sively passed through the entire 6-year dairy production cycle (Fig. 1). The number of initial female calves and heifers was estimated account- ing for calf death losses (7.8 and 1.8% between 0 and 3 and 3-6 months, respectively; USDA, 2010). The sex ratio of new-born calves was as- sumed to be 51:49 (male:female). Veal calves were grown as young veal calves for 3 months (40-107 kg) before being finished for 3.5 months (107-273 Consistent with the average replacement rate (31.9%) and number of lactations (2.75) of cows in Quebec (Valacta, 2009), 25% of the cows were removed from the herd (i.e., as- sumed to be sent to the abattoir) at the beginning of their third lactation with the remainder slaughtered after giving birth to their fourth calf. In commercial practice, however, the culling of COWS would be spread out over the production cycle. A single culling time was used to represent the average number of lactations, replacement rate, and dynamics of the herd to simplify the calculations, recognizing this approach would have no impact on the net emissions over the entire cycle.

3: Diets fed to calves were similar between CS- and BS-dairy produc- tion systems and mainly consisted of milk replacer for young females and veal calves, grass-legume hay for older female calves, and corn grain for older veal calves (Mc Geough et al., 2012; Table 1). Heifers and non-lactating (dry) COWS received a diet containing grass-legume hay supplemented with either corn or barley silage, and dietary crude protein (CP) content and yield of enteric CH4 (Ym, defined as MJ of CH4 produced per MJ feed DM consumed) were predicted from diet composition (NRC, 2001; Little et al., 2013). Lactating animals were of- fered a corn or barley silage-based diet where ingredient composition and Ym were those reported by Benchaar et al. (2014). Because corn si- lage was lower in CP (-2% of DM) and NDF -15%) content, the CS- based diet contained additional sources of nitrogen (N; corn gluten feed and urea) and fiber (soybean hulls) in order to obtain similar nutri- ent content between diets.

The effect of corn and barley silage digestibility on total GHG emis- sions at the system level was evaluated by changing the TDN content of silage offered to cows and heifers. For each silage, the TDN content was varied from low (LD; 65 and 55% for CS and BS, respectively), medi- um (MD; 70 and 60% for CS and BS, respectively), and high (HD; 75 and 65% for CS and BS, respectively) (NRC, 2001; Table 2). Diet CP content and Ym were assumed to be constant regardless of silage digestibility.

2.1.3. Milk production and number of animals

Daily milk production from the study of Benchaar et al. (2014) was used for cows fed HD corn (37.0 kg milk/day) and MD barley (31.9 kg milk/day) silage, respectively, because the TDN content of these diets was similar to their associated predicted TDN (NRC, 2001). Milk produc- tion of cows fed diets higher or lower in TDN content was estimated as- suming a reduction of 0.85 kg milk per 1% decrease in TDN content of diet (NRC, 2001; Table 2). Milk production of primiparous COWS was as- sumed to be 6.8% less than that of multiparous COWS (Valacta, 2009). Milk fat and protein content was based on Benchaar et al. (2014) and was assumed to be constant regardless of the physiological stage of an- imals and TDN content of silage; no consistent relationship has been established between silage DM digestibility and milk composition (Bal et al., 1997; Ferraretto and Shaver, 2012; Khan et al., 2012). Fat- and protein-corrected milk (FPCM) was calculated as recommended by the International Dairy Federation (IDF, 2010): FPCM (kg/d) = milk (kg/d) X [0.1226 X fat (%) + 0.0776 X protein (%) + 0.2534].

The number of lactating COWS was adjusted such that the total FPCM production per cycle was similar among silage sources and TDN con- tents, with a target FPCM of 2 million kilograms FPCM per cycle and per farm (353,000 kg FPCM per year and per farm; Table 2).

(2014) was used for cows fed HD corn (37.0 kg milk/day) and MD barley (31.9 kg milk/day) silage, respectively, because the TDN content of these diets was similar to their associated predicted TDN (NRC, 2001). Milk produc- tion of cows fed diets higher or lower in TDN content was estimated as- suming a reduction of 0.85 kg milk per 1% decrease in TDN content of diet (NRC, 2001; Table 2). (2014) and was assumed to be constant regardless of the physiological stage of an- imals and TDN content of silage; no consistent relationship has been established between silage DM digestibility and milk composition (Bal et al., 1997; Ferraretto and Shaver, 2012; Khan et al., 2012). Fat- and protein-corrected milk (FPCM) was calculated as recommended by the International Dairy Federation (IDF, 2010): FPCM (kg/d) = milk (kg/d) X [0.1226 X fat (%) + 0.0776 X protein (%) + 0.2534].

The number of lactating COWS was adjusted such that the total FPCM production per cycle was similar among silage sources and TDN con- tents, with a target FPCM of 2 million kilograms FPCM per cycle and per farm (353,000 kg FPCM per year and per farm; Table 2).

To determine the quantity of feed grown or purchased, total quantities of offered feed were calculated per ingredient based on number of animals and days on-feed, diet com- position, DM intake predicted by Holos, moisture content, feeding wast- age and losses during harvest for crops produced on-farm only (Table 3). Intake was predicted based on energy requirement of the an- imals (maintenance, activity, lactation, pregnancy or gain) and TDN of the diets (Little et al., 2013). Crops produced on-farm were not irrigated, and corn and barley crops (silage and grain) received herbicide and lime (Table 4). All the manure was applied once a year to fields and the quan- tity of manure exceeded crop N and phosphorus (P) requirements such that no inorganic fertilizer was needed.

For each silage source, the effect of biomass yield on land surface area requirement and GHG emissions was estimated using low (LY), medium (MY), and high (HY) yields. For CS, yields corresponded to the minimum, average and maximum yields in the Napierville area (Quebec, Canada) between 1995 and 2013 (La Financière Agricole du Québec, 2014). Because statistics for barley silage grown in Quebec were not available, yields were estimated based on the growing season of the local area.

18: <header>2.3. Quantification of GHG emissions and co-product allocation </header>
Quantification of GHG sources followed a "cradle-to-farm gate" ap- proach, i.e. from growing feed until animals left the farm (Fig. 2). Total

GHG emissions (kg CO2-equivalent; kg CO2-eq) were estimated using Holos (Version 2.1.1.), a whole-farm software program developed by Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada (http://www.agr.gc.ca/holos-ghg; Little et al., 2013). Briefly, GHG emissions were calculated with a yearly time step model based on Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) methodologies and with algorithms modified to reflect Canadian farming practices. They were expressed as CO2-equivalent (CO2-eq), by multiplying the amount of gas produced with its 100-year global warming potential (CO2 = 1, CH4 = 28, N20 = 265; IPCC, 2014).
19: Carbon dioxide emissions from electricity consumption for the dairy operation (194 kg CO2/cow/year; Vergé et al., 2007) were taken into ac- count. Manure storage generated direct and indirect CH4 and N20 emis- sions (IPCC, 2006; Mc Geough et al., 2012). Direct gaseous emissions were calculated from environmental parameters, handling system, and manure composition. Indirect N20 emissions varied according to manure composition, volatilization and leaching fractions. Manure composition was mainly based on the TDN and CP contents of the diet (Little et al., 2013). As the boundaries of the system were at the farm gate, GHG emissions from animal transport and post-production pro- cessing of milk and meat were not included (Beauchemin et al., 2010).
20: <header>2.3.2. GHG emissions from feed production and crop management </header>

Forage and grain production generated CO2 during cropping opera- tions (fuel consumption) and lime, herbicide, and manure application, direct N20 during N degradation from manure and crop residues, and indirect N20 during N leaching and volatilization (Table 5; Mc Geough et al., 2012). The GHG emissions associated with lime and herbicide ap- plication included CO2 emissions from their production and transporta- tion to the farm as well as lime degradation on field. Emissions for seed production and manufacture of equipment (machinery and infrastruc- ture) were excluded as they were assumed to have little effect on the comparison of scenarios. As grass- and forage-seeded lands were regarded as temporary grasslands, it was considered that they did not provide permanent capture and storage of carbon (C). Consequently, C sequestration was not accounted for.

Carbon dioxide emissions from processing and truck transportation of purchased feed were also taken into account, as well as energy re- quired to dry grain produced on-farm (Table 5). As much as possible, the C footprint of purchased feed was based on data generated in North America (Adom et al., 2013), to accurately reflect the Canadian situation.

The carcass yield was assumed to be similar be- tween cows and calves (60% of live weight; Mc Geough et al., 2012). Total protein produced on-farm was calculated from milk and meat (17.32%; USDA, 2011) protein contents. Energy produced from milk was calculated from NRC (milk energy (MJ/kg) = 4.184 X [0.0929 X fat (%) + 0.0547 X protein (%) + 0.192]; NRC, 2001) and energy produced from meat was calculated from USDA (12.18 MJ/kg meat; USDA, 2011).

Four coproduct allocation methodologies were assessed (IDF, 2010). In the first one, the dairy production unit was considered as solely re- sponsible for all GHG emissions, which were then fully assigned to milk. However, as both meat and milk were produced on-farm, eco- nomic, physical and protein allocations were also assessed (Gerber et al., 2010; Mc Geough et al., 2012). For economic allocation, the

proportion of GHG assigned to milk and meat production represented the percentage of the farm income coming from each product, given that the price of 1 kg of milk and meat from culled COWS and veal calves was set at $0.80 (5-year average price in Quebec; Canadian Dairy Information Centre, 2015), $2.29, and $5.07 (5-year average price in Canada; Agriculture and Agri-food Canada, 2014), respectively. For physical allocation, a factor representing the repartition of feed energy required for an animal to produce milk and meat was calculated based on the equation recommended by IDF (2010) and using data associated with the simulated farms (allocation factor = 1 - 6.04 [total liveweight produced per cycle / total FPCM produced per cycle]). This factor indicated the proportion of total GHG emissions to be allocated to milk. Finally, given that the main goal of the dairy sector is to generate edible protein for human food, the proportion of GHG assigned to milk and meat production was determined according to the amount of pro- tein provided by both products (protein allocation; Gerber et al., 2010).

30: 3.1. Effect of corn silage and barley silage of medium digestibility (MD) on greenhouse gas intensity

When MD silages were compared, total GHG emissions were re- duced by 13% with CS compared to BS (Table 6). As a consequence, total GHG emissions expressed per cow, FPCM (kg), carcass weight (kg), total protein produced (kg), and total energy produced (MJ) were lower for the CS dairy production system regardless of the alloca- tion method (Table 7). This difference can be attributed to lower emis- sions of enteric CH4 -19%), manure CH4 -11%), manure N20 ( 15%), crop direct N20 -36%), crop and manure indirect N20 25%), and energy CO2 -28%). The lower total CH4 emission of the CS dairy production system was primarily attributed to the enteric CH4- mitigating effect of CS and the greater feed efficiency of lactating cows fed CS that allowed for a smaller number of COWS on the farm required to produce the same amount of milk. In both systems, enteric CH4 accounted for 49% of total GHG emissions on average (Fig. 3), which is in the range of the literature (35-49%; Phetteplace et al., 2001; Vergé et al., 2007; Ogino et al., 2008; Flysjö et al., 2011; Mc Geough et al., 2012; O'Brien et al., 2012; Jayasundara and Wagner-Riddle, 2013). The large contribution of enteric CH4 to total GHG emissions at the farm scale highlights the importance of developing CH4 mitigation practices for reducing the environmental impact of ruminant production systems.

Due to higher biomass yield, CS cultivation required a smaller land surface than BS (192 ha versus 361 ha) and generated less N residues re- ducing N20 emissions. This land surface difference also explained the lower CO2-eq intensity of the BS dairy production system when total GHG emissions were expressed per hectare (Table 7). However, increas- ing cropping area needed as in the BS feeding system reduces the area of
land available to produce other crops that may be sold generating farm income. Spared land could also be used for wildlife habitat or other eco- system services.

The proportion of GHG from processing and transportation of feed was 9% higher (percentage units) with CS compared with BS (Fig. 3). Corn silage contains less protein and fiber than barley silage and conse- quently, corn gluten feed, urea and soybean hulls were required in CS to reach appropriate CP and fiber levels. These three purchased feeds alone were responsible for 69% of GHG emissions among purchased feeds for CS (16% of total GHG emissions) which partly offset the reduction in other GHG. The importance of purchased feed for the net GHG balance of dairy production systems was previously highlighted by Refsgaard et al. (1998), who reported a higher energy cost for milk produced on a conventional farm purchasing feed supplements compared to an or- ganic farm growing its own feed.

When GHG emissions were fully allocated to milk, the GHG intensity of the CS dairy production system (1.18 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM corre- sponding to 1.16 kg CO2-eq/kg milk) was well within the range (0.92- 1.53 kg CO2-eq/kg milk) of reported values for dairy systems using corn silage as the main forage source (Phetteplace et al., 2001; Vergé et al., 2007; Capper et al., 2008; Ogino et al., 2008; Capper et al., 2009; Flysjö et al., 2011; Mc Geough et al., 2012; O'Brien et al., 2012). Variabil- ity among studies may be related to different assumptions and calcula- tions chosen for each LCA, as well as the diversity of geographical areas inducing variations in agronomic parameters (biotic and abiotic fac- tors). When exclusively examining Canadian data for similar dairy sys- tems using CS, Vergé et al. (2007) and Jayasundara and Wagner- Riddle (2013) reported a GHG intensity of 1.02 and 1.18 kg CO2-eq/kg milk, respectively (100% allocation to milk). Several factors may explain why GHG intensity slightly differs among studies. For instance, in the present work the 100-year GWP of CH4 and N20 was based on IPCC

This difference can be attributed to lower emis- sions of enteric CH4 -19%), manure CH4 -11%), manure N20 ( 15%), crop direct N20 -36%), crop and manure indirect N20 25%), and energy CO2 -28%). The lower total CH4 emission of the CS dairy production system was primarily attributed to the enteric CH4- mitigating effect of CS and the greater feed efficiency of lactating cows fed CS that allowed for a smaller number of COWS on the farm required to produce the same amount of milk. In both systems, enteric CH4 accounted for 49% of total GHG emissions on average (Fig. 3), which is in the range of the literature (35-49%; Phetteplace et al., 2001; Vergé et al., 2007; Ogino et al., 2008; Flysjö et al., 2011; Mc Geough et al., 2012; O'Brien et al., 2012; Jayasundara and Wagner-Riddle, 2013). The large contribution of enteric CH4 to total GHG emissions at the farm scale highlights the importance of developing CH4 mitigation practices for reducing the environmental impact of ruminant production systems.

Due to higher biomass yield, CS cultivation required a smaller land surface than BS (192 ha versus 361 ha) and generated less N residues re- ducing N20 emissions. This land surface difference also explained the lower CO2-eq intensity of the BS dairy production system when total GHG emissions were expressed per hectare (Table 7). However, increas- ing cropping area needed as in the BS feeding system reduces the area of
land available to produce other crops that may be sold generating farm income. Spared land could also be used for wildlife habitat or other eco- system services.

The proportion of GHG from processing and transportation of feed was 9% higher (percentage units) with CS compared with BS (Fig. 3). Corn silage contains less protein and fiber than barley silage and conse- quently, corn gluten feed, urea and soybean hulls were required in CS to reach appropriate CP and fiber levels. These three purchased feeds alone were responsible for 69% of GHG emissions among purchased feeds for CS (16% of total GHG emissions) which partly offset the reduction in other GHG. The importance of purchased feed for the net GHG balance of dairy production systems was previously highlighted by Refsgaard et al. (1998), who reported a higher energy cost for milk produced on a conventional farm purchasing feed supplements compared to an or- ganic farm growing its own feed.

When GHG emissions were fully allocated to milk, the GHG intensity of the CS dairy production system (1.18 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM corre- sponding to 1.16 kg CO2-eq/kg milk) was well within the range (0.92- 1.53 kg CO2-eq/kg milk) of reported values for dairy systems using corn silage as the main forage source (Phetteplace et al., 2001; Vergé et al., 2007; Capper et al., 2008; Ogino et al., 2008; Capper et al., 2009; Flysjö et al., 2011; Mc Geough et al., 2012; O'Brien et al., 2012). Variabil- ity among studies may be related to different assumptions and calcula- tions chosen for each LCA, as well as the diversity of geographical areas inducing variations in agronomic parameters (biotic and abiotic fac- tors). When exclusively examining Canadian data for similar dairy sys- tems using CS, Vergé et al. (2007) and Jayasundara and Wagner- Riddle (2013) reported a GHG intensity of 1.02 and 1.18 kg CO2-eq/kg milk, respectively (100% allocation to milk). (2014) whereas former studies used values from IPCC (1996) (310 for N20 and 21 for CH4) or IPCC (2007) (298 for N20 and 25 for CH4). In ad- dition, in our study N requirement of crops was considered to be ful- filled by organic N (manure) produced on-farm, so that no synthetic fertilizer was applied. Earlier studies assumed application of both sources of fertilizer, increasing the associated CO2 emissions from pro- duction and transportation of inorganic N, and increasing the N20 emis- sions from N degradation. Between-study comparisons of LCA may also be affected by differences in herd structure and milk production, ma- nure handling system or yield of enteric CH4 emissions. Overall, these differences indicate that comparison of scenarios within a study using constant assumptions and algorithms is more relevant. Under the conditions of this study, the CS dairy production system had lower environmental impact when assessed as GHG emissions than the BS dairy production system. 45: <header>3.2. Effect of forage yield and digestibility on greenhouse gas intensity </header>

Within each forage source, forage yield did not impact total GHG emissions, but increasing yields reduced the land required and therefore increased GHG expressed per hectare by 15 and 21% for CS and BS re- spectively, regardless of the TDN content of silages (data not shown). However, this increase in GHG intensity could be offset by the benefits of the increased land available for other ecosystem services. Increasing plant maturity at harvest increases starch content of grain-based silages, decreases fiber digestibility of stover, with the net effect of increasing TDN content and dairy milk production (Johnson

et al., 1999; Ferraretto and Shaver, 2012). In Canada, silage TDN content usually varies from 65 to 75% of DM for corn, and from 55 to 65% of DM for barley (Baron et al., 2000; NRC, 2001). The effect of feeding CS or BS of different TDN content on GHG intensity at a dairy farm scale has not been reported previously. For both forage sources, increasing TDN con- tent from LD to HD reduced total GHG emissions by 17 and 18% for CS and BS dairy production systems, respectively (Table 6). This positive environmental effect resulted from the combined effect of the lower number of animals and DMI required to produce the same amount of milk. For CS and BS systems respectively, 6 and 7 additional lactating cows were required in LD diet compared to HD diet. The lower DMI for lactating cows fed HD diet (24.8 kg DMI/day for HD diet versus 25.5 kg DMI/day for LD diet in the CS system; 26.2 kg DMI/day for HD diet versus 27.2 kg DMI/day for LD diet in the BS system) can be ex- plained by the higher energy content of this diet, resulting in a better feed efficiency of cows (as indicated by the DMI/FPCM ratio) and a lower daily CH4 emissions despite a constant Ym. Moreover, lower DMI can also decrease manure production and therefore lower manure emissions. Overall, increasing diet TDN content from LD to HD reduced enteric CH4 16% and - 17% in CS and BS systems, respectively), ma- nure CH4 23% and - 24% in CS and BS system, respectively), manure N20 19% and - 20% in CS and BS systems, respectively), crop direct N20 19% in both systems), crop indirect N20 19 and - 20% in CS and BS systems, respectively), energy CO2 15% and - 16% in CS
Fig. Greenhouse gas proportion in dairy production systems using corn (A)- or barley (B)-based feeding systems with silages of medium digestibility as main forage source for lactating dairy cows.

and BS systems, respectively) and purchased feed CO2 15% and 17% in CS and BS systems, respectively). Moreover, the required land surface was also reduced with forages of higher TDN content ( 16% and - 17% in CS and BS systems, respectively). As a conse- quence, increasing forage TDN content reduced GHG intensity whatever the expression unit and allocation method (Table 7). In practice, this ef- fect may be less marked, as increasing diet digestibility may increase Ym (Hatew et al., 2016). Similarly, with a meta-analysis approach, Sauvant et al. (2011) reported a positive relationship between CH4 emissions and the intake of digestible organic matter. To verify the impact of the Ym value, we conducted a post-study evaluation by changing Ym for cows fed CS of medium TDN content from 5.7 to 5.3% of GE intake. We found that GHG intensity was only lowered from 1.18 to 1.15 kg CO2- eq/kg FPCM (100% allocation to milk), which is still greater than the GHG intensity of CS with high TDN content. The final aspect of this study was an evaluation of the threshold TDN content of barley silage needed for BS dairy production systems to have a lower GHG intensity than CS systems. When combining the effect of forage source and silage digestibility, CS dairy systems always had lower GHG intensities than BS systems, except when comparing sys- tems producing barley silage of high digestibility with systems produc- ing corn silage of low digestibility. Producing forages with high digestibility can be challenging, particularly for corn silage grown in northern climates due to a relatively short growing season (Baron et al., 2000). Increasing maturity at harvest increases biomass yield and improves the nutritive value of whole plant silage through increased grain content in cobs (corn silage) or kernels (barley silage) and re- duced fiber content in stover (corn silage) or straw (barley silage), al- though fiber digestibility of silage declines with maturity (Johnson et al., 1999). However, delaying maturity at harvest to maximize grain fill- ing is often not possible for corn silage grown in northern climates be- cause of the onset of frost near the end of the growing season. Furthermore, the DM content of the whole plant at harvest must be in the optimum range (32 to 42%) for silage making. Plant genetics also af- fect nutritive value of silage, although the environmental conditions for plant growth and harvest timing have a greater impact on yield and di- gestibility of corn silage (Owens, 2014). Johnson et al. (1999) plotted the positive relationship between corn silage digestibility and growing degree units (defined as the sum of mean daily temperature above a threshold temperature over a period of time), which explained 56% of the variation in corn silage digestibility. Adequate growing season to ensure grain maturation of corn hy- brids is not available in all regions of Canada. As corn development is more heat-dependent than small grain production (Baron et al., 2000), dairy farms must adapt their forage production to local agronomic and environmental conditions to be highly productive. The use of short-sea- son corn hybrids requiring fewer corn heat units may improve whole plant digestibility in areas with short growing season (Baron et al., 2000). Consideration must be given to yield potential, maturity at har- vest, standability, disease and insect resistance, and drought tolerance (Owens, 2014). In addition, operating cost per hectare of corn silage production can be significantly greater (up to 50% more) than that of small grain production (e.g. barley or triticale; Baron et al., 2000), thus achieving maximum yield and digestibility is also critical for farm income.

The lower DMI for lactating cows fed HD diet (24.8 kg DMI/day for HD diet versus 25.5 kg DMI/day for LD diet in the CS system; 26.2 kg DMI/day for HD diet versus 27.2 kg DMI/day for LD diet in the BS system) can be ex- plained by the higher energy content of this diet, resulting in a better feed efficiency of cows (as indicated by the DMI/FPCM ratio) and a lower daily CH4 emissions despite a constant Ym. Moreover, lower DMI can also decrease manure production and therefore lower manure emissions. Overall, increasing diet TDN content from LD to HD reduced enteric CH4 16% and - 17% in CS and BS systems, respectively), ma- nure CH4 23% and - 24% in CS and BS system, respectively), manure N20 19% and - 20% in CS and BS systems, respectively), crop direct N20 19% in both systems), crop indirect N20 19 and - 20% in CS and BS systems, respectively), energy CO2 15% and - 16% in CS
Fig. Greenhouse gas proportion in dairy production systems using corn (A)- or barley (B)-based feeding systems with silages of medium digestibility as main forage source for lactating dairy cows.

and BS systems, respectively) and purchased feed CO2 15% and 17% in CS and BS systems, respectively). Moreover, the required land surface was also reduced with forages of higher TDN content ( 16% and - 17% in CS and BS systems, respectively). As a conse- quence, increasing forage TDN content reduced GHG intensity whatever the expression unit and allocation method (Table 7). In practice, this ef- fect may be less marked, as increasing diet digestibility may increase Ym (Hatew et al., 2016). Similarly, with a meta-analysis approach, Sauvant et al. (2011) reported a positive relationship between CH4 emissions and the intake of digestible organic matter. To verify the impact of the Ym value, we conducted a post-study evaluation by changing Ym for cows fed CS of medium TDN content from 5.7 to 5.3% of GE intake. We found that GHG intensity was only lowered from 1.18 to 1.15 kg CO2- eq/kg FPCM (100% allocation to milk), which is still greater than the GHG intensity of CS with high TDN content. The final aspect of this study was an evaluation of the threshold TDN content of barley silage needed for BS dairy production systems to have a lower GHG intensity than CS systems. When combining the effect of forage source and silage digestibility, CS dairy systems always had lower GHG intensities than BS systems, except when comparing sys- tems producing barley silage of high digestibility with systems produc- ing corn silage of low digestibility. Producing forages with high digestibility can be challenging, particularly for corn silage grown in northern climates due to a relatively short growing season (Baron et al., 2000). Increasing maturity at harvest increases biomass yield and improves the nutritive value of whole plant silage through increased grain content in cobs (corn silage) or kernels (barley silage) and re- duced fiber content in stover (corn silage) or straw (barley silage), al- though fiber digestibility of silage declines with maturity (Johnson et al., 1999). However, delaying maturity at harvest to maximize grain fill- ing is often not possible for corn silage grown in northern climates be- cause of the onset of frost near the end of the growing season. Furthermore, the DM content of the whole plant at harvest must be in the optimum range (32 to 42%) for silage making. Plant genetics also af- fect nutritive value of silage, although the environmental conditions for plant growth and harvest timing have a greater impact on yield and di- gestibility of corn silage (Owens, 2014). Johnson et al. (1999) plotted the positive relationship between corn silage digestibility and growing degree units (defined as the sum of mean daily temperature above a threshold temperature over a period of time), which explained 56% of the variation in corn silage digestibility. Adequate growing season to ensure grain maturation of corn hy- brids is not available in all regions of Canada. As corn development is more heat-dependent than small grain production (Baron et al., 2000), dairy farms must adapt their forage production to local agronomic and environmental conditions to be highly productive. The use of short-sea- son corn hybrids requiring fewer corn heat units may improve whole plant digestibility in areas with short growing season (Baron et al., 2000). Consideration must be given to yield potential, maturity at har- vest, standability, disease and insect resistance, and drought tolerance (Owens, 2014). In addition, operating cost per hectare of corn silage production can be significantly greater (up to 50% more) than that of small grain production (e.g. barley or triticale; Baron et al., 2000), thus achieving maximum yield and digestibility is also critical for farm income.
48: <header>3.2. Effect of forage yield and digestibility on greenhouse gas intensity </header>

Within each forage source, forage yield did not impact total GHG emissions, but increasing yields reduced the land required and therefore increased GHG expressed per hectare by 15 and 21% for CS and BS re- spectively, regardless of the TDN content of silages (data not shown). However, this increase in GHG intensity could be offset by the benefits of the increased land available for other ecosystem services. Increasing plant maturity at harvest increases starch content of grain-based silages, decreases fiber digestibility of stover, with the net effect of increasing TDN content and dairy milk production (Johnson

et al., 1999; Ferraretto and Shaver, 2012). In Canada, silage TDN content usually varies from 65 to 75% of DM for corn, and from 55 to 65% of DM for barley (Baron et al., 2000; NRC, 2001). The effect of feeding CS or BS of different TDN content on GHG intensity at a dairy farm scale has not been reported previously. 49: Corn based dairy production systems reduce GHG intensity of milk production compared with BS, due to a reduction of enteric CH4 emis- sions. However, with CS an increase of CO2 emissions associated with production and transportation of purchased protein sources can occur, but an overall reduction in GHG intensity still takes place. This high- lights that studies aiming at developing dietary CH4-mitigating strate- gies must be accompanied by a complete assessment of on-farm GHG emissions, especially if these strategies change agronomic practices

such as the cropping system. Moreover, because increasing silage di- gestibility reduces GHG intensity, local environmental conditions deter- mining forage quality have to be addressed to optimize ruminant production systems.
